segments.purgatorio-6-g23: Vidi is the passato remoto (simple past) first-person singular of vedere — 'I saw'. 'conte Orso' is a noun phrase (title + name). 'l’anima divisa' uses the past participle divisa predicatively with anima (feminine) meaning 'the soul separated'. 'dal' is the contraction da + il ('from the'), linking corpo suo (his body) as the source. The two per phrases ('per astio e per inveggia') state the cause/reason: per here means 'by/through' and inveggia is an archaic/poetic form related to 'invidia' (envy).
segments.purgatorio-6-g24: Com' is the contracted form of come ('as'/'like'); e' here is an archaic/poetic spelling with apostrophe standing for egli ('he'), and dicea is the imperfect tense of dire ('he was saying' / 'he used to say'), so the whole phrase is a parenthetical 'as he was saying'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g25: Non negates the clause: 'not'. Per here means 'for' or 'because of' so 'not for ...'. Colpa is a feminine noun 'fault/guilt', and commisa is a past participle (feminine to agree with colpa) from commettere — 'committed'. Together: 'not for any fault committed.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g26: Pier da la Broccia is an appositive identification; da = 'from' (here part of a name/form of origin). Dico is present-tense first person singular 'I say' (or 'I mean'), used parenthetically to clarify that the speaker is naming Pier da la Broccia.
segments.purgatorio-6-g27: E = 'and'. Qui means 'here' or 'at this point'. Proveggia is an archaic/poetic present tense (from proveggiare) meaning 'provides', 'takes measures' or 'acts to provide', and it will link to the following noun phrase as its subject or object depending on syntax: 'and here (someone) provides/acts'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g28: Mentr' is a contracted form of mentre ('while/whereas'). È is the present tense of essere ('is'). Di qua is a locative phrase meaning 'on this side' or 'here' — di = 'of/from' used with qua to indicate position ('on this side'). The clause gives temporal/location information: 'while she is on this side'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g29: La donna di Brabante is a straightforward noun phrase: the article + donna ('lady/woman') + di Brabante ('of Brabant' — a regional designation). This phrase supplies the person who acts (or for whom provision is made) in the previous clause.
segments.purgatorio-6-g30: Sì che introduces a result or purpose clause ('so that'). Però here functions as a reinforcing adverb ('therefore/so') before the negative purpose. Non sia uses the present subjunctive of essere ('may not be' / 'is not to be'), typical after sì che when expressing a desired/resulting state. 'Di peggior greggia' means 'of a worse flock' (greggia = flock), i.e. 'so that she may not belong to a worse group.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g31: Come is temporal here ("when/as"); libero fui is passato remoto (literary past) meaning "I was freed" with libero as a predicate adjective. Da tutte quante is a reinforced prepositional phrase "from all of" (quante intensifies tutte). Quell' ombre is an archaic/elided form of quelle ombre ("those shades"); the relative clause che pregar pur ch’altri prieghi uses the infinitive pregar in a reported/characterizing clause "who prayed" and introduces a subordinate that-clause (ch’) with prieghi (a subjunctive form) meaning "that others pray."
segments.purgatorio-6-g32: Sì che = "so that/so as" introducing the result/purpose. S' is the reflexive/impersonal si; avacci is a literary verb form meaning roughly "come to pass for them / befall them" (here: "may come to them"). Lor = to them; divenir sante is infinitive + adjective "to become holy/saints," sante agreeing in gender/number with the implied people.
segments.purgatorio-6-g33: Io is the emphatic subject pronoun "I." Cominciai is passato remoto of cominciare meaning "I began (to speak)." The colon after it introduces Dante's direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-6-g34: El par = impersonal/neuter "it seems" (el = neuter 'it'); par is present "seems/appears." Che introduces the content clause. Tu is the subject "you," mi is the indirect object "to me," and nieghi is the present subjunctive/indicative of niegare used here as "you deny/refuse (me)."
segments.purgatorio-6-g35: O is the vocative particle "O" (address). Luce mia is a possessive vocative "my light," a familiar/pet name for the addressee; mia agrees with luce.
segments.purgatorio-6-g36: Espresso is a past participle here used impersonally/passively: "is/it is expressed." In alcun testo = "in some text/passage." Che introduces the subordinate clause whose subject is decreto del cielo ("decree of heaven"); orazion (prayer) is the noun and pieghi is the finite verb (here best read as a 3rd-person form meaning "bends" or "is bent by"), so the clause says that prayer bends (or moves) the decree of heaven. Word order and archaic inflections are poetic.
segments.purgatorio-6-g37: E = "and." Questa gente = "these people/this group." Prega is present indicative "pray" (3rd sg.); pur here means "only/merely" (literary sense). Di questo = "for this/with regard to this," i.e. they pray for this purpose alone.
segments.purgatorio-6-g38: Sarebbe is the conditional of essere ("would be"); dunque = "therefore/then." Loro is the dative/indirect object "to them/their." Vana = adjective "vain/empty." The clause asks rhetorically whether their hope would therefore be vain.
segments.purgatorio-6-g39: O introduces an alternative question: "or... ?" Non m'è = short for non mi è, literally "is not to me" ("is not to me... clear"). 'L detto tuo = "your saying/what you said" (detto as a noun from dire). Ben manifesto = "well/clearly manifest." The whole line: "or is not your saying to me clearly manifest?"
segments.purgatorio-6-g40: A single punctuation mark ending the previous speech (period).
segments.purgatorio-6-g41: Ed = "and." Elli is an archaic/poetic form meaning "he/that one" (subject of the speech). A me = "to me" marks the indirect object; the colon introduces the direct quotation.
segments.purgatorio-6-g42: La mia scrittura = "my writing/text" (scrittura = text or statement). È piana = "is plain/straightforward/clear," piana an adjective indicating clarity here.
segments.purgatorio-6-g43: E = "and." La speranza di costor = "the hope of those people." Non falla = literally "does not fail/decieve" – falla here means "fails/lets down" (so negated: "does not fail"). The whole clause: "and their hope does not fail."
segments.purgatorio-6-g44: Se ben = "if indeed/if properly." Si guarda is a reflexive/impersonal present meaning "one looks/considers" or "is considered." Con la mente sana = "with a sound/healthy mind." Together: "if it is well considered with a sound mind."
segments.purgatorio-6-g45: Ché = "for/because." Cima di giudicio = "the summit/height of judgment." Non s'avvalla = reflexive verb meaning "does not slump/sink/yield" (i.e. the top of judgment does not give way). The clause explains why hope is not in vain.
segments.purgatorio-6-g46: Perché introduces a purpose/result clause here: "so that/that"; foco d'amor is the subject "the fire (ardor) of love." Compia is present subjunctive of compiere ("accomplish/complete") used to express what love might accomplish; in un punto = "in a single point/moment."
segments.purgatorio-6-g47: Ciò che = "that which/what." De’ sodisfar = an archaic periphrasis meaning "must/ought to satisfy" (de’ here marks obligation before the infinitive sodisfar). Chi qui s'astalla = "who here lingers/tarries" (s'astalla = reflexive verb meaning to stay or delay). Together: "what those who linger here must satisfy."
segments.purgatorio-6-g48: E là dov' io fermai cotesto punto = "and there where I stopped/fixed this point/limit." Dov' is abbreviated dove (where); fermai is passato remoto "I fixed/stopped;" cotesto is an archaic demonstrative "this/that."
segments.purgatorio-6-g49: Non s'ammendava = imperfect reflexive/impersonal of ammendare: "it was not (could not be) amended/corrected." The negation plus imperfect implies an ongoing inability to remedy the defect.
segments.purgatorio-6-g50: Per pregar = "by praying/through prayer" (gerund/infinitival sense of purpose/means). Difetto = "defect/failing" (the thing that could not be remedied). Perché introduces the reason: 'because the prayer (il priego) was disjoined/separated from God' (era disgiunto = "was separated").
segments.purgatorio-6-g80: Pur is an adversative adverb meaning 'still' or 'yet' and precedes the subject for emphasis. 'Virgilio' is the subject. 'si trasse' is passato remoto of the reflexive verb 'trarre' (trarsi), literally 'drew himself' or 'withdrew himself toward'; the reflexive pronoun 'si' marks that the action affects the subject and 'a lei' is the directional phrase 'toward her'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g81: 'pregando' is a present participle used adverbially here: 'begging' or 'entreating'. It introduces a finite subordinate clause with 'che' plus the imperfect subjunctive 'mostrasse' (see below). 'ne' is a clitic referring back to the matter/place previously under discussion ('of it'/'about it'). 'la miglior salita' is a nominal phrase 'the best ascent/way up' (miglior used as superlative before the noun).
segments.purgatorio-6-g82: 'e' = 'and'. 'quella' = 'that one' (the woman previously mentioned). 'non rispuose' is passato remoto of 'rispondere' meaning 'did not answer'; subject is 'quella'. 'al suo dimando' = 'to his question/request', with 'suo' referring back to the speaker (Virgil).
segments.purgatorio-6-g83: 'ma' = 'but'. 'di nostro paese e de la vita' are two objects of inquiry, 'about our country/homeland and about our life'. 'ci' is the clitic object 'to us', and '’nchiese' (here the past tense of 'chiedere') means 'asked'. (Note: the lemma/sense list provided for 'nchiese' does not include the verbal sense 'asked', so the sense index is set to -1.)
segments.purgatorio-6-g84: 'e' = 'and'. '’l' is the elided article + noun 'il duca' = 'the guide' (here Virgil). 'dolce' is an epithet 'sweet'/'dear'. 'incominciava' is the imperfect 'began/was beginning' and introduces the direct speech that follows (here the single word 'Mantüa' which he begins to speak).
segments.purgatorio-6-g85: A single punctuation mark (period) marking the end of the printed line or clause; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-6-g86: A single punctuation mark (period) marking the end of the printed line or clause; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-6-g87: A closing quotation mark and comma marking the transition from the duke's speech to the narrative; no lexical items to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-6-g88: 'e' = 'and'. 'l’ombra' = 'the shade' or 'ghost' (here the spirit/ghost of a person); the phrase sets the subject of the following clause.
segments.purgatorio-6-g89: 'tutta' = 'entirely/wholly'. 'in sé' = 'in itself' or 'withdrawn into itself' and 'romita' is an adjective meaning 'solitary/remote' (here describing the shade as inward, secluded). Together they describe the shade's posture or demeanor.
segments.purgatorio-6-g90: 'surse' is passato remoto of 'sorgere' meaning 'rose' or 'arose'. 'ver’' is a contraction of 'verso' meaning 'toward' (the provided sense list does not capture this directional sense, so its sense index is -1). 'lui' is the indirect object 'him'. 'del loco ove pria stava' = 'from the place where it had been before', with 'stava' imperfect/past tense of 'stare' meaning 'was situated'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g91: 'dicendo' is the present participle of 'dire' used adverbially: 'saying' (introducing the reported speech that follows).
segments.purgatorio-6-g92: '«O' is the vocative interjection 'O' = 'O'/'Oh' used to address someone. 'Mantoano' = 'Mantuan' (a person from Mantua), the addressee of the speech.
segments.purgatorio-6-g93: 'io' = 'I'. 'son' is the present of 'essere' (here 'I am'). 'Sordello' is the proper name. 'de la tua terra' = 'of your land'/'from your land', with 'tua' 'your' and 'terra' 'land/homeland'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g67: Prima che introduces a temporal subordinate clause meaning 'before'. sie is an archaic/subdialectal form of the subjunctive of essere (modern Italian sia) used after prima che to indicate an action not yet occurred. Là sù is an adverbial phrase (là = 'there', sù = 'up/above') placed after the verb for poetic cadence.
segments.purgatorio-6-g68: Tornar vedrai is a poetic inversion in which the infinitive tornar ('to return') precedes the finite future vedrai ('you will see') and expresses 'you will see (him) return.' Colui che introduces the relative clause 'the one who...'. Già is temporal ('by then/already'); si cuopre is reflexive present indicative ('he covers himself' or 'cloaks himself') and de la costa uses the archaic preposition de for di + the feminine noun costa ('slope/mountainside').
segments.purgatorio-6-g69: Sì che introduces a result clause ('so that'). ’ suoi shows an elision (apostrophe) but functions as the possessive suoi ('his/its') modifying raggi ('rays'). Tu romper non fai is a poetic placement where the infinitive romper ('to break') appears before the finite verb fai ('you do/make'); with the negative non it yields 'you do not break / you cannot break' (i.e. you will be unable to break his rays).
segments.purgatorio-6-g70: Ma is a simple adversative particle ('but'). Vedi here is an exhortative/present imperative use ('behold' or 'look'), addressed to the listener. Là is the locative adverb 'there'. Un' anima is the indefinite article + noun ('a soul'); the comma and che introduce the following relative clause 'who...'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g71: Posta is the past participle of porre used predicatively with anima ('placed' or 'standing'), agreeing in the feminine. Sola soletta is a pleonastic doublet for emphasis meaning 'all alone' (sola = 'alone', soletta = diminutive/emphatic 'all alone').
segments.purgatorio-6-g72: Inverso noi is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward us' (inverso here = 'towards/turned to'). Riguarda is the present indicative 3rd singular of riguardare meaning 'looks at' or 'turns her gaze to' (not 'concerns' here). Word order places the directional phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-6-g73: Quella is the demonstrative subject 'that one' (feminine, referring back to the soul). Ne is a clitic referring back to the matter/context just spoken (here roughly 'to/for us' or 'with regard to that'); it marks an indirect relation to the verb. ’nsegnerà is future indicative 3rd singular of insegnare (the initial letter is elided by the apostrophe) used in the sense 'will show/point out' rather than only 'teach'. La via più tosta = 'the more arduous way' (più + adjective to form comparative).
segments.purgatorio-6-g74: Venimmo is the passato remoto (simple past) first-person plural of venire ('we came'/'we reached'), used narratively. A lei is the prepositional phrase 'to her' (lei as object). The colon marks direct address that follows.
segments.purgatorio-6-g75: O is the vocative particle (exclamation 'O'/'Oh') addressing the soul. Anima lombarda is a nominative vocative phrase 'Lombard soul' (lombarda agrees in gender).
segments.purgatorio-6-g76: Come is exclamative 'how'. Ti stavi is the imperfect second-person singular of stare used as copula ('you stood / you were') and gives a descriptive past sense. Altera e disdegnosa are coordinate adjectives describing her demeanor ('haughty and disdainful'). Nel mover de li occhi is a prepositional phrase 'in the movement of the eyes' (nel = in + il; de li = archaic di + definite article). Onesta e tarda are adjectives modifying that gesture—'dignified (honourable) and slow.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g77: Ella is the subject pronoun 'she'. Non ci dicëa is imperfect indicative 'she said to us not / she did not say to us'—dicëa is marked with a trema to show vowel separation but is simply the imperfect of dire. Alcuna cosa with the negative non yields 'not any thing' = 'nothing.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g78: Ma is 'but'. Lasciavane is an imperfect form of lasciare with an enclitic ne attached (archaic ordering) meaning 'she let [us] go' or 'she permitted [us] to go on'; the clitic reflects a pronominal element. Gir is the infinitive 'to go/turn/wander' and completes the verb lasciava + infinitive construction.
segments.purgatorio-6-g79: Solo sguardando is a present participle phrase 'only gazing' (solo = 'only' or 'alone'; sguardando = 'gazing'). A guisa di leon quando si posa is a simile: 'in the manner of a lion when it rests'—guisa = 'manner', leon = 'lion', si posa is reflexive present indicative 'it rests/settles.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g51: Veramente is an adverb meaning 'truly/indeed' that opens and reinforces the sentence. The prepositional phrase a così alto sospetto ('at / in face of such a high/lofty suspicion') places the cause or circumstance before the verb; alto is used in the figurative sense 'lofty'. Non + clitic + verb (non ti fermar) is the negative imperative/prohibitive construction: non + object clitic ti + verb stem fermar (an archaic/poetic truncation of fermare) yields 'do not stop (yourself) / do not be stopped'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g52: Se introduces a conditional 'if'. Quella ('that one' / 'she' / 'that') refers back to the previously mentioned person; nol is the contracted form of non + lo (not + it/him), placed before the clitic ti and the verb dice ('says/tells') in the old clitic order: nol ti dice = 'she does not tell you it'. Che introduces the subordinate clause reporting content. Lume fia uses fia (an older/fixed future form related to essere) as 'will be' and lume in the sense 'light/illumination'; tra 'l vero e lo ’ntelletto is a locative/comparative 'between the True (truth) and the intellect'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g53: Non so is the simple present 'I do not know.' Se introduces an indirect question 'if/whether (you) understand.' ’ntendi is dialectal/poetic elision of intendi (the initial vowel lost and marked by an apostrophe), meaning 'you understand.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g54: Io dico di Beatrice: io 'I' is emphatic subject, dico is the present 'I say' (here 'I mean / I refer to'), and di Beatrice means 'of/about Beatrice' — 'I mean Beatrice' or 'I am speaking of Beatrice.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g55: Tu la vedrai is future 'you will see her' (vedrai = 2nd person singular future). The clitic la is the object 'her' (placed between subject and verb as in Italian). Di sopra is an adverbial phrase 'from above' or 'above' indicating location.
segments.purgatorio-6-g56: In su la vetta is a stacked prepositional phrase meaning 'up on the summit' (in + su intensify upward position), with la vetta 'the summit' as the noun phrase. Di questo monte is a normal genitive phrase 'of this mountain' specifying which summit.
segments.purgatorio-6-g57: Ridere e felice: ridere is used in a descriptival (participial/infinitival) sense 'laughing' and is coordinated with the adjective felice 'happy', giving the result 'laughing and happy.' The verbless linking is typical of poetic equivalence: 'you will see her laughing and happy.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g58: E io simply introduces the speaker: 'And I' (I reply / I say). The conjunction E links his speech to what preceded.
segments.purgatorio-6-g59: Segnore is an archaic/spelling variant of Signore, vocative 'Lord' / 'Sir' used here as polite address to the guide; the -e ending reflects older orthography.
segments.purgatorio-6-g60: Andiamo is 1st person plural present used hortatively 'let us go' or 'we go on.' A maggior fretta is a manner phrase 'with greater haste' (a + comparative + noun), so the clause means 'let's go with greater haste.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g61: Ché (because/for) introduces the reason. Già here is emphatic 'already/by now.' Non m'affatico is reflexive present 'I do not tire myself' (mi affatico), with the clitic contracted to m'. Come dianzi means 'as before/earlier' — dianzi is an adverb 'previously.' Altogether: 'for I am not already as tired as before.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g62: E vedi omai che introduces an observation 'and you see now that...' Vedi is 2nd person singular present 'you see/notice.' Omai = 'now/by now.' Che introduces the content clause: 'that il poggio (the hill) l'ombra (the shadow) getta (casts)'; note the older word order where the object l'ombra precedes the verb getta ('casts').
segments.purgatorio-6-g63: Noi andrem is a first-person-plural verb form used to indicate future movement 'we shall go / we will go on' (anderem is a poetic/older form). Con questo giorno innanzi literally 'with this day before (us)' means 'during the remainder of this day / with this day ahead of us' — con + temporal noun expresses accompaniment or the time frame.
segments.purgatorio-6-g64: Rispuose is the past historic (antecedent) 3rd-person singular 'he answered.' It marks the reporting of the speaker's reply.
segments.purgatorio-6-g65: Quanto più potremo omai is a purposive/limiting phrase: quanto più = 'as much as / insofar as', potremo is future 'we will be able', and omai 'now/by now' — together 'as much as we shall be able now' or 'as far as we can at present.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g66: Ma introduces the contrast 'but.' ’L fatto è d’altra forma = 'the matter/thing is of another form/kind' (fatto used as 'matter' or 'fact/situation'). Che non stanzi is an idiomatic negative: stanzi here functions in the sense 'to remain / to allow delay' (an older usage), so the phrase means roughly 'and it will not permit delay / it will not allow us to stay.' The whole clause signals that circumstances prevent lingering.
segments.purgatorio-6-g142: This segment contains only a full stop (punctuation). In the poem it functions as a pause/line break; there are no lexical items or grammatical forms to analyze.
segments.purgatorio-6-g143: "Vieni" is the second-person singular imperative (addressing 'you') meaning 'come!'; here it introduces an exhortation. "a" before an infinitive marks purpose or direction and links to the following verb (so "a veder" = 'to see'). "veder" is the infinitive (poetic clipped form of "vedere"). "la gente" is the noun phrase 'the people' (la + gente). "quanto" is an exclamative meaning 'how'/'how much' modifying the clause that follows. "s'" is the reflexive/reciprocal clitic (from "si") attached before the verb, and "ama" is the present indicative of "amare"; together "s'ama" means 'they love one another' (reciprocal action). The clause therefore literally orders the addressee to come in order to see how strongly the people love each other.
segments.purgatorio-6-g144: "e" is the simple conjunction 'and' linking this clause to the previous exhortation. "se" would normally be the conditional 'if' introducing the condition, but that sense is not represented in the provided sense list (see senses). "nulla" here functions as negation meaning 'not at all'/'nothing' modifying the idea that pity moves you. "di noi" literally 'of us' (= 'for us') modifies "pietà" (pity for us). "pietà" is the noun 'pity/mercy'. "ti" is the indirect/object pronoun 'you' (the person affected). "move" is the present indicative of "movere" used causatively/impersonally here: 'moves you'/'impels you' (i.e. 'affects you emotionally'). The whole clause means 'and if no pity for us moves you (i.e. if you feel no pity for us)'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g145: "a" introduces an infinitival phrase that follows the impersonal construction 'ti vien' (literally 'it comes to you') and marks purpose/infinitive complement ('to be ashamed'). "vergognar" is the infinitive form (poetic/clipped for "vergognare" or used impersonally for "vergognarsi") meaning 'to be ashamed' here; with "ti" it indicates that shame will affect you. "ti" is the clitic pronoun 'you'. "vien" is the present indicative (from "venire") used impersonally here: 'it comes'/'occurs' (i.e. 'shame comes upon you'). "de" = 'of'/'about' linking to the following noun. "la tua fama" = 'your fame/reputation' (la + tua + fama). Together: 'shame about your reputation will come upon you.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g146: "E" is the conjunction 'and' continuing the address. "se" again would be the conditional 'if' (sense not present in the provided list). "licito" is an impersonal adjective/compound meaning 'permissible' (here 'if it is permitted' or 'if I may'). "m'" (mi) is the dative/clitic 'to me' ("licito m'è" = 'it is permitted to me'). "è" is the present indicative of "essere" ('is'), completing the impersonal construction 'it is permitted to me'. The phrase behaves like a polite preface: 'And if it is permitted to me...' (allowing the speaker to ask or say what follows).
segments.purgatorio-6-g147: "o" is the vocative interjection 'O' (addressing someone directly). "sommo" is an adjective 'highest'/'supreme' modifying the vocative "Giove". "che" is the relative pronoun 'who' introducing a relative clause. The form listed as "fosti" should be the perfect of "essere" ('you were'); here it means 'who were (on) earth' — the provided sense list lacks that meaning. "in terra" = 'on earth'; "per noi" = 'for us'; "crucifisso" is the adjective/past participle 'crucified', describing Giove in this poetic, Christianized appellation. The whole segment is a formal apostrophe: 'O highest Jove, who were crucified on earth for us,'
segments.purgatorio-6-g148: "son" is the contracted present of "sono" ('are') introducing the question 'are ... ?'. "li" is the archaic/dialectal article 'the' (plural) used before "giusti occhi". "giusti" here is an adjective meaning 'righteous' or 'just' qualifying "occhi"; the provided sense list does not include that adjectival sense. "occhi" literally 'eyes' (or 'gaze') is the subject. "tuoi" is the possessive 'your' agreeing with occhi. "rivolti" is the past participle of "rivolgere" used adjectivally/passive: 'turned' or 'directed'. "altrove" means 'elsewhere'. With the interrogative word order the clause asks whether God's righteous eyes are turned away: 'are your righteous eyes turned elsewhere?'
segments.purgatorio-6-g94: A single semicolon marking a pause; it separates this clause from the preceding speech and functions as punctuation only.
segments.purgatorio-6-g95: e = 'and' (simple coordinating conjunction). l’un l’altro is a rhetorical reciprocal expression (literally 'the one the other') functioning as direct objects referring to two parties; abbracciava is the imperfect indicative, 3rd person singular (literally 'embraced' or 'was embracing'), so the clause reads 'and the one embraced the other.' The singular verb form is stylistic but refers to a reciprocal action.
segments.purgatorio-6-g96: Ahi is an interjection ('ah'/'alas') expressing lament; serva here is predicative and ought to be read as 'subject/enslaved' (archaic/poetic adjective/noun use), modifying Italia (Italy).
segments.purgatorio-6-g97: di + ostello = 'hostel of' (di = 'of'); dolore is a noun meaning 'sorrow' so ostello is described as a 'hostel/inn of sorrow' — a figurative apposition to 'Italia.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g98: A noun phrase in apposition: nave ('ship') + sanza (archaic for 'without') + nocchiere ('helmsman/pilot'); in gran tempesta places the ship 'in a great storm' (gran is the apocopated form of grande).
segments.purgatorio-6-g99: non introduces a contrastive negation: 'not a donna di province' — donna here is 'lady' (respectable woman, metaphorically the country's dignity), di province = 'of provinces' (i.e. mistress/ruler of regions). Word order is emphatic: non + noun phrase.
segments.purgatorio-6-g100: ma = 'but' (contrast); bordello is a blunt predicate noun meaning literally 'brothel' (here figuratively 'scene of disorder/vice'), completing the antithesis with 'not a lady... but a brothel.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g101: Quell' is an apocopated demonstrative ('that' = quella) used before a vowel; anima gentil is the noun phrase 'noble soul.' fu is the past of essere (here passato remoto/preterite form 'was'); così presta is a predicate adjective phrase meaning 'so ready' or 'so quick to act' (presta = 'ready/eager' in this usage).
segments.purgatorio-6-g102: sol here means 'only/merely' (archaic solo/sol); per = 'by/through'; lo dolce suon = 'the sweet sound' (lo = definite article before s+consonant/cons.). de = archaic form of di ('of'); la sua terra = 'his homeland' (terra as 'land'). The whole prepositional phrase gives the motive ('solely because of the sweet sound of his homeland').
segments.purgatorio-6-g103: di fare = the infinitive 'to make/do' introduced by di (governing infinitive after adjectives like presta); al cittadin suo = 'for/to his fellow-citizen' (al = a + il); quivi = archaic 'there'; festa = 'feast/celebration.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g104: e ora = 'and now'; in te = 'in you' (i.e. within your territory); non stanno = 'do not stand/remain' (stanno, 3rd plural present of stare); sanza = archaic 'without'; guerra = 'war' or 'strife'; li vivi tuoi = 'your living ones/your living people' (li is the archaic masculine plural article placed before the noun: subject follows the verb for emphasis).
segments.purgatorio-6-g105: e l’un l’altro si rode = 'and one [the] other gnaws (at) himself/each other': l’un l’altro = reciprocal phrase, si indicates reciprocal action ('each other'), rode is present indicative of rodere ('to gnaw', used figuratively 'to corrode/vex'). di quei ch’un muro e una fossa serra = 'over those whom a wall and a ditch enclose' (ch' = che = 'who/which'; serra is present indicative 'closes/encloses').
segments.purgatorio-6-g106: Cerca is an imperative (2nd person singular) of cercare here addressed to 'misera' — a command: 'search/look for.' It can also be read as present indicative in other contexts, but the vocative that follows makes the imperative sense clear.
segments.purgatorio-6-g107: misera is a vocative adjective ('wretched one') addressing the subject (Italy) directly; the comma marks direct address.
segments.purgatorio-6-g108: intorno da le prode = 'around/from the headlands/coasts' (intorno = 'around'); le tue marine = 'your maritime [forces/coasts]' (marine = maritime/coastal), so the whole phrase means 'around your coasts.' Word order places the adverbial phrase before the noun for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-6-g109: e poi ti guarda in seno = 'and then look (imperative guardare) into your breast/bosom' (ti guarda can be read as an imperative 'look thou' following the previous imperative; in seno = 'in the breast/heart' = figurative introspection).
segments.purgatorio-6-g110: s'alcuna = contraction of se alcuna (if any); parte = 'part/portion'; in te = 'in you'; di pace gode = 'enjoys peace' (gode = 3rd person singular present of godere = 'enjoys'). The clause is a conditional interrogative: 'see whether any part of you enjoys peace.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g1: Quando is the temporal adverb 'when'. Si + parte is a pronominal/impersonal use of partire (present tense) meaning 'breaks up/comes to an end' (literally 'one parts/it parts'). de la is an older form of di + la ('of the') and zara names the game.
segments.purgatorio-6-g2: Colui che perde is a relative clause 'he who loses' (colui = 'the one, he who'). Si riman dolente uses the reflexive/impersonal si with riman (archaic spelling of rimane) to mean 'remains' or 'is left' in a sorrowful state.
segments.purgatorio-6-g3: Repetendo is a present gerund ('by repeating'); it expresses a concurrent or attendant action. Le volte is the direct object of the gerund and means 'the turns/rounds' (volte = turns/repetitions).
segments.purgatorio-6-g4: E connects clauses. Tristo is a predicate adjective 'sad/sorrowful'. Impara is 3rd person singular present of imparare 'learns' — here indicating the result or lesson learned in sorrow.
segments.purgatorio-6-g5: Con l’altro introduces accompaniment ('with the other' — i.e. the winner). Se ne va is the pronominal verb andarsene 'goes away' (se = reflexive particle, ne indicates movement away); tutta la gente is the subject/group that leaves.
segments.purgatorio-6-g6: Qual is an indefinite/distributive pronoun 'some/one'; va is present 'goes'; dinanzi is an adverb 'in front, ahead'. Together: 'some go ahead'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g7: E qual introduces another case ('and some'); di dietro is a locution 'from behind'; il prende = 'takes him' (prende is present indicative 'takes/seizes'). The clause lists different positions people take.
segments.purgatorio-6-g8: E qual dallato gives a third case ('and some, from the side'); dallato is an adverb/phrase 'from the side'. Li is the object pronoun 'them' (here referring to people/turns), si is pronominal, and reca (3rd sg. present of recare) + a mente forms the phrase recarsi a mente 'bring/come to mind, recall'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g9: El (archaic for 'egli') is the 3rd person subject pronoun 'he'. Non negates the clause. S' (si) + arresta is the pronominal verb 'stops/halts' in the present — 'he does not stop'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g10: E connects to the next statement. Questo e quello ('this one and that one') are objects of intende; intende (3rd sg. present of intendere) here means 'attends to/cares for' or 'gives attention to' rather than 'intend'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g11: A cui is a relative phrase 'to whom'; porge (3rd sg. present of porgere) means 'offers/extends'; la man = 'the hand' (la mano contracted to man). The clause describes a gesture toward someone.
segments.purgatorio-6-g12: Più non is a negation pattern meaning 'no longer/any longer'. Fa (3rd sg. present of fare) + pressa ('haste/urgency') = 'makes haste' so the negative means 'he no longer hurries'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g13: E così 'and so/thus' introduces the result. Da la calca 'from the crush/crowd' uses da + article (old separation of preposition and article). Si difende is the pronominal reflexive 'defends himself/wards off'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g14: Tal is a demonstrative/adverb 'such' or 'so'. Era io is the verb 'I was' (imperfect of essere) with inverted order for emphasis. In quella turba spessa: quella = 'that', turba = 'crowd/throng', spessa = 'thick/dense' (adjective after noun).
segments.purgatorio-6-g15: Volgendo is a present gerund of volgere 'turning/directing' and a + loro 'to them' marks direction — 'turning to them'. The gerund links a concurrent action to the main clause.
segments.purgatorio-6-g16: E qua e là is a simple adverbial phrase 'here and there' (qua = 'here', là = 'there'), with e as simple connective.
segments.purgatorio-6-g17: La faccia is the direct object 'the face' (la = article, faccia = face/expression). It's presented alone as an object phrase following the adverbial one.
segments.purgatorio-6-g18: E promettendo is a gerund 'and by promising/while promising'. Mi is the object pronoun 'me'. Sciogliea is an archaic imperfect form (sciogliea = scioglieva) of sciogliere 'loosen/release' (3rd sg.), so 'he/it would free me' or 'I was freed' depending on subject; da essa = 'from it' (essa referring back to the crowd).
segments.purgatorio-6-g19: Quiv' (quivi) 'there' + era is the imperfect/past 'was' — locative past. L’Aretin = 'the Aretine' (a man from Arezzo). Che introduces a relative clause: da le braccia fiere 'by the fierce arms/hands' (da indicates agency/cause here), di Ghin di Tacco names the agent. Ebbe la morte uses ebbe (passato remoto of avere) literally 'had/get' + la morte = 'met death' (idiomatic 'was killed').
segments.purgatorio-6-g20: E connects to a second item. L’altro 'the other' + ch' (che) introduces its relative clause. Annegò is passato remoto 'drowned'; correndo is a gerund 'while running' (or 'running'), in caccia = 'in a hunt' (hunting scene).
segments.purgatorio-6-g21: Quivi 'there' + pregava is imperfect 'was praying' (3rd person). Con le mani sporte is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'with hands outstretched' — sporte is a past-participle/adjectival form describing the hands. The personal name follows (Federigo Novello).
segments.purgatorio-6-g22: E quel da Pisa = 'and that one/from Pisa' (quel = 'that man'). Che introduces the relative clause; fé is passato remoto of fare ('made/caused') used causatively with the infinitive parer 'to appear' (fé parer = 'caused to appear'); lo buon Marzucco forte is the object + predicate ('the good Marzucco [to be] strong').
segments.purgatorio-6-g158: Rifiutan is an archaic/poetic third-person plural present indicative (modern Italian rifiutano) meaning 'they refuse'. Lo is the definite article used before the adjective+noun phrase; comune modifies incarco. Incarco is a masculine singular noun meaning 'charge' or 'responsibility'. The word order is subject (Molti) + verb + direct object.
segments.purgatorio-6-g159: Ma is the coordinating conjunction 'but'. Il popol tuo places the article before the noun phrase (popol is the shortened poetic form of popolo) with the possessive tuo following the noun for emphasis. Solicito here is an adjective meaning 'eager' or 'prompt'. Risponde is third-person singular present 'responds/answers'. Sanza is the archaic form of senza ('without'), and chiamare is the infinitive 'to call/summon' used after sanza to mean 'without being summoned'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g160: E is the coordinating 'and' linking the previous clause to this verb. Grida is third-person singular present indicative 'shouts/cries out' describing what the people do; the colon introduces direct speech.
segments.purgatorio-6-g161: I’ is the contracted poetic form of io ('I') with an apostrophe. Mi is the first-person reflexive/object clitic. Sobbarco is first-person singular present 'I take upon myself' (sobbarcarsi) — here an emphatic exclamation of volunteering to bear the burden.
segments.purgatorio-6-g162: Sentence-final punctuation: a period marking the end of the line/sentence.
segments.purgatorio-6-g163: Or functions as 'now' (or 'then') introducing the statement. Ti is the indirect object/clitic 'you'. Fa is third-person singular present of fare 'to make', so fa lieta means 'makes (you) glad'; lieta is a feminine adjective agreeing with an implied feminine noun (the city, cittÃ ) rather than with ti.
segments.purgatorio-6-g164: Ché is a conjunction meaning 'because/for'. Tu hai is the present indicative 'you have'. Ben is an adverb/adjectival intensifier here ('good' / 'ample') modifying what follows. Onde, used idiomatically in Dante to mean 'grounds' or 'whence', is an archaic/figurative use not represented by the literal sense 'wave' listed in the provided senses.
segments.purgatorio-6-g165: Tu is a repeated emphatic subject pronoun. Ricca is a feminine adjective 'rich' agreeing with the implied feminine noun (the city), used here in a series of brief appositions describing the addressee's favourable condition.
segments.purgatorio-6-g166: This is a parallel construction: tu con ... e tu con ... each tu is emphatic; con is the preposition 'with'. Pace means 'peace' or 'peacefulness', and senno is a noun 'sense'/'prudence' (here 'good sense' or 'wisdom'). The rhythm repeats for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-6-g167: S’io is the contracted/poetic form of se io (if I); this elision is typical of older/poetic Italian. Dico is first-person singular present 'I say' or 'I tell'. Ver is the poetic form of veritÃ (truth) and functions as the direct object. The contraction S’ in the provided senses list does not match the conditional 'se' here, so its sense is marked -1.
segments.purgatorio-6-g168: L’ is the elided definite article before effetto (l'effetto). Effetto is the subject/noun 'effect' or 'outcome'. Nol is the clitic form of non + lo (not it) placed before the verb; nasconde is third-person singular present of nascondere 'to hide' or 'to conceal'. The clause thus says the effect does not hide it.
segments.purgatorio-6-g124: Ch’ is the contracted form of che; here it opens an exclamatory/rhetorical clause ('what' or 'that') and is followed by the auxiliary avete before the pronoun tu (inversion common in older/poetic Italian). Avete tu e ’l tuo padre sofferto = 'have you and your father suffered/experienced'. Sofferto is the past participle completing the compound verb avere + sofferto.
segments.purgatorio-6-g125: Per introduces the cause or means ('by/through'); cupidigia is a noun 'greed'; di costà is a prepositional phrase 'of/over there' with costà (an adverb or deictic) indicating place; distretti is a past-participial form used predicatively ('pressed/compelled'), agreeing in sense with the sufferers and linking the cause to the previous verb.
segments.purgatorio-6-g126: Che begins a result/consequence clause ('that/so that'); l' (l) + giardin is 'the garden'; de lo ’mperio = 'of the empire' (de + lo is an older form of di + il); sia is the present subjunctive of essere (used here to express result or present potential: 'may be/be'), and diserto is intended as a past-participle/adjective 'deserted' describing the garden (note: the lemma given is irregular, so sense is not listed in the supplied options).
segments.purgatorio-6-g127: Vieni is an imperative or hortative 'come!' addressed to the second person; a + veder is the infinitive construction meaning 'to see' (Vieni a veder = 'come to see'); Montecchi and Cappelletti are plural proper names/family names (Cappelletti here is a surname plural, not 'little chapels').
segments.purgatorio-6-g128: Two further family names in apposition (Monaldi e Filippeschi); the plural forms name groups/families and function as direct objects of the infinitive veder or as examples to be seen.
segments.purgatorio-6-g129: Uom is the archaic form of uomo ('man'); sanza is an archaic preposition meaning senza ('without'); cura is a noun 'care/anxiety' — together 'a man without care' (i.e. unconcerned).
segments.purgatorio-6-g130: Color here is a demonstrative plural 'those (people)'; già means 'already' or 'by now'; tristi is the adjective 'sad' agreeing with color: 'those already sad'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g131: E connects the clauses; questi is a demonstrative 'these' (referring to other people just mentioned); con + sospetti = 'with suspicions' or 'full of suspicion' describing their mental state — sospetti is a plural noun.
segments.purgatorio-6-g132: Vien (variant spelling of vieni) is an imperative 'come!' addressing the addressee; in poetry vocatives and commands often come alone for emphasis.
segments.purgatorio-6-g133: Crudel is a vocative adjective 'cruel' directly addressing the person; in older Italian adjectives sometimes stand alone as a form of address.
segments.purgatorio-6-g134: Vieni is repeated as an imperative 'come!' for force and rhythm; the comma and repetition mark rhetorical urgency.
segments.purgatorio-6-g135: E 'and' links clauses; vedi is 2nd-person singular imperative/indicative of vedere here meaning 'see/observe'; la pressura is the noun phrase 'the pressing/crowding/pressure'; d’ is the contracted form of di before the plural article; i tuoi gentili = 'your nobles/highborn people' (gentili = nobles/highborn), so the whole phrase means 'see the pressure exerted by your nobles.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g136: E 'and' again links; cura here is the imperative form of curare ('take care of / attend to / mind') addressed to the same addressee (notice the verb form identical to the noun in other contexts); lor is the archaic clitic for loro ('their'); magagne is a plural noun 'faults/defects' — 'mind their faults' or 'attend to/see their faults.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g137: E 'and' links to a future-vision clause; vedrai is the future 2nd-person singular of vedere ('you will see'); Santafior is a place-name; com’ is the contracted form of come ('how'); è is present indicative of essere ('is'); oscura is an adjective 'dark/obscure' describing Santafior.
segments.purgatorio-6-g138: Vieni a veder as before: imperative 'come to see'; la tua Roma is 'your Rome'; che introduces a relative clause 'that/which'; piagne is 3rd-person singular present 'weeps/laments'; vedova e sola are predicate adjectives 'widowed and alone', describing Rome.
segments.purgatorio-6-g139: E 'and' links clauses; dì e notte is the idiom 'day and night' (here dì is a noun 'day' rather than the verb meaning 'say' — the provided lemma senses list treats dì as a verb, so the correct nominal sense is not in that list); chiama is 3rd-person singular present of chiamare 'calls out' (Rome personified calling).
segments.purgatorio-6-g140: Cesare mio is a direct address in the quoted speech: Cesare ('Caesar/emperor') + mio ('my') in possessive vocative position.
segments.purgatorio-6-g141: Perché introduces the interrogative 'why'; non is the negation; m’ (mi) is the clitic object 'me'; accompagne is 3rd-person singular present subjunctive/indicative form of accompagnare in older spelling but here functions as present indicative 'accompany' in the question 'why do you not accompany me?'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g149: The line opens with O meaning 'or' (posing an alternative or question). È preparazion places the verb before the noun (poetic inversion): è = 'is' and preparazion = 'preparation.' The relative clause che ne l’abisso del tuo consiglio fai modifies preparazion: ne l’ (archaic form for nell’) + abisso = 'in the abyss,' del tuo consiglio = 'of your counsel/plan,' and fai is 2nd-person singular present 'you make.' Per alcun bene gives the purpose 'for some good,' and the closing phrase in tutto de l’accorger nostro scisso uses in tutto = 'completely/entirely,' de l’accorger nostro = 'from our noticing/awareness' (accorger here used as a nominalized notion of perception), with scisso (past participle) 'torn/split off' describing the separation from our perception.
segments.purgatorio-6-g150: Ché = 'for/because' introduces the reason. Le città d’Italia = 'the cities of Italy' (d’ = di before a vowel), with tutte piene postpositive ('all full') agreeing in gender/number with città. Son is the archaic/poetic form of sono ('are'), and di tiranni means 'with/filled by tyrants' (di expressing 'full of').
segments.purgatorio-6-g151: The second clause links with e = 'and.' Un Marcel diventa = 'becomes a Marcel,' where diventa is 3rd-person singular present 'becomes.' Ogne (archaic ogn-) villan = 'every peasant/boor' (adjective before the noun), and the relative clause che parteggiando viene uses the gerund parteggiando 'taking sides' (describing his action) with viene 'comes/arrives' — together 'every peasant who comes by taking sides becomes a Marcel.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g152: Fiorenza mia is a vocative address 'my Florence' (noun + possessive) with intimate word order (noun before possessive), used to address the city directly.
segments.purgatorio-6-g153: Ben puoi esser contenta is a modal construction: ben = 'well,' puoi (2nd-singular of potere) + esser (infinitive essere) = 'you can be,' and contenta is the predicate adjective agreeing with Fiorenza (feminine). Di questa digression = 'about/with this digression' (di introduces the object of contentment). Che non ti tocca is a relative clause 'that does not touch/concern you' (tocca used idiomatically as 'concern').
segments.purgatorio-6-g154: Mercé here functions as 'thanks to'/'by virtue of' rather than literal 'mercy.' Del popol tuo = 'of your people' (del = di + il). Che si argomenta is a relative clause: si is the reflexive/reciprocal clitic ('to themselves'/'with one another') and argomenta (argomentare) means 'discuss/argue' — 'your people who argue among themselves.'
segments.purgatorio-6-g155: Molti is the plural subject 'many'; han (archaic han = hanno) is 'have'; giustizia in cuore is the noun phrase 'justice in (their) heart(s)' meaning a sense of justice in their feelings. The phrase describes disposition rather than public action.
segments.purgatorio-6-g156: E tardi scocca uses the metaphor scocca ('shoots/strikes/releases,' as of an arrow) with tardi 'late' — 'it shoots late.' Per non venir sanza consiglio a l’arco is a causal/explanatory phrase: per non venir = 'because (of) not coming,' sanza = 'without' (archaic), consiglio = 'counsel/advice,' and a l’arco = 'to the bow' (idiomatically meaning 'to the point of action' — they won't go to the bow without counsel), so the whole clause says justice is slow because people won't act without counsel.
segments.purgatorio-6-g157: Ma = 'but.' Il popol tuo = 'your people' with the article common in poetic diction. L’ (here a clitic object 'it') ha = 'have it' (l' refers back to giustizia), and in sommo de la bocca = 'at the very top/edge of the mouth' (sommo = highest/top), an idiom meaning they merely hold justice at their lips (i.e., they talk about it but do not act).
segments.purgatorio-6-g169: Two proper names coordinated by e (and). Lacedemona is the Italianized/feminine form for Lacedaemon (Sparta). The comma marks the end of the noun phrase that the following relative clause will modify.
segments.purgatorio-6-g170: Che is the relative pronoun referring back to the two cities ('which'). Fenno is an archaic past form meaning 'they made/caused' (equivalent to modern fecero). L’antiche leggi = 'the ancient laws' (l' = the). Furon is an archaic past of essere, 'they were'; sì civili = 'so civic/so civil' (i.e. civic-minded or civilized).
segments.purgatorio-6-g171: Fecero is past of fare 'they made/gave'. Al is the contraction a + il and here begins the prepositional phrase al viver bene = 'toward/to the living well' where viver (infinitive) functions as a noun-like expression 'living/conduct of life' and bene qualifies it. Un picciol cenno = 'a small sign/hint'. Verso di te is a directional phrase 'toward you' (verso + prepositional complement with the pronoun).
segments.purgatorio-6-g172: Che here is another relative pronoun 'who/which' referring to the addressee ('you'). Fai is present tense 'you make/do'. Tanto is an adverb 'so' (to emphasize degree), sottili is plural adjective 'subtle/fine', and provedimenti is plural noun 'provisions/measures' (i.e. political measures or precautions).
segments.purgatorio-6-g173: Ch' is short for che 'that', introducing the result clause. A mezzo novembre is a temporal phrase meaning 'by/around mid-November' (a + mezzo = at/by the middle of). Non giugne is present-tense negated 'does not reach/arrive' (giugne = giunge). Quel che = 'that which/what', and tu d'ottobre fili = 'you spin/produce in October' (d'ottobre = 'of October' = 'in October'); fili here functions as a verb in the original sense 'you spin' (see senses note).
segments.purgatorio-6-g174: Quante volte is an exclamatory interrogative phrase 'how many times' / 'how often'. Volte is plural of volta, used as 'times/occasions'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g175: Del (di + il) introduces the genitive 'of the time'. Che is a relative pronoun 'that/which', and rimembre is a present-tense verb (1st person) meaning 'I remember'—so the phrase reads 'of the time that I remember'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g176: A simple nominal item in a list: legge = 'law'. The comma separates list items.
segments.purgatorio-6-g177: Another list item: moneta refers to the coinage/currency (money).
segments.purgatorio-6-g178: Officio = an office or official post, e = and, costume = custom/manners. Hai tu mutato is a present perfect construction with auxiliary avere before the subject tu (inverted for emphasis): 'have you changed' (hai = have, mutato = past participle 'changed').
segments.purgatorio-6-g179: E = and. Rinovate is a past participle (feminine/plural to agree with membre) meaning 'renewed' (used here exclamatorily). Membre = 'limbs' (plural of membrum) used figuratively for bodies or members; the exclamation implies repeated renewal or reconstitution of the city's members/institutions.
segments.purgatorio-6-g180: E = and. Se begins a conditional 'if'. Ben (bene) = 'well' (adverb). Ti ricordi = 'you remember' (ricordi is present indicative 2nd person). E vedi lume = 'and you see the light' (vedi = you see; lume can be literal 'light' or figurative 'mental light/clear vision'). The clause is conditional: 'And if you remember well and see clearly'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g181: Vedrai is future tense 'you will see'. Te is the object pronoun 'yourself'. Somigliante a = 'resembling'. Quella inferma = 'that sick woman' (inferma is feminine adjective used substantively here). The relative clause che non può trovar posa in su le piume = 'who cannot find rest upon the feathers'; può is modal 'can' and trovar is the infinitive 'to find'; posa = 'rest' and in su is an archaic doubled preposition 'on'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g182: Ma = but. Con + infinitive (con dar) can mean 'by giving/with giving'—here con dar volta = 'by turning' (dar + volta forming an idiom 'to turn'). Suo dolore = 'her sorrow' (suo agreeing in gender/number), scherma is present indicative 'she shields/guards' (from schermare), so the clause means 'but by turning she shields her sorrow' (i.e. she wards it off by turning away).
segments.purgatorio-6-g111: Che val is a rhetorical impersonal question meaning "what avails / what good is" (che = "what", val from valere = "avail/be of use"). Perché here introduces a concessive subordinate clause with the imperfect subjunctive racconciasse (literary/older form), meaning roughly "even if he mended/adjusted." Ti is the indirect object "to you/for you." Il freno = "the bridle/bit/rein" (literal equipage term). Iustinïano is the proper name Justinian, placed after the object as the agent of the subordinate verb.
segments.purgatorio-6-g112: Se is the conditional "if." La sella is the saddle/seat (la = definite article). È is the present indicative of essere "is." Vòta is an archaic spelling of vota/vuota, meaning "empty"; the question asks whether the seat is empty, which makes the previous effort pointless.
segments.purgatorio-6-g113: Sanz' is a contracted form of senza "without." Esso is the neuter/masculine pronoun "it" referring back to the thing just mentioned (the empty saddle/absence). Fora is a literary/archaic form of the verb essere in a subjunctive/conditional sense (compare modern fosse/serrebbe) meaning roughly "would be" or "were." La vergogna meno places the noun "the shame" with the comparative/degree word meno "less," giving the result clause "the shame would be less."
segments.purgatorio-6-g114: Ahi = interjection "Alas." Gente is the vocative "people" (collective). Che introduces a relative clause: dovresti (conditional of dovere) + esser (infinitive) devota (adjective) = "who ought to be devout." Note devota agrees with the collective noun.
segments.purgatorio-6-g115: E = "and." Lasciar = infinitive "to let/allow." Seder is the infinitive "to sit" (literary form). Cesare is the logical subject of the infinitive ("let Caesar sit"); in la sella = "in the seat/saddle" (metonymy for rule/power).
segments.purgatorio-6-g116: Se bene = "if indeed/if properly." Intendi is present "you understand/perceive." Ciò che = "that which;" Dio ti nota = "God notes/marks to you/points out to you" (nota as present verb 'notes/tells'). Overall: "if you properly understand what God points out to you."
segments.purgatorio-6-g117: Guarda = imperative "look/see." Come = "how/as." Esta = archaic demonstrative "this." Fiera = "beast/animal" used figuratively; è fatta fella = "has been made/wrought wicked/fierce" (fella = wicked). Per non esser corretta da li sproni = "because it is not corrected by the spurs" (i.e. not disciplined).
segments.purgatorio-6-g118: Poi che = "since/after" (introducing cause). Ponesti is passato remoto 2nd person singular "you put/placed." Mano a la predella = "hand on the predella" (predella = the base/footboard of the altar; metaphorically, meddling with church authority). The clause accuses the addressees of causing the evil by their action.
segments.purgatorio-6-g119: This begins with a vocative 'O Alberto tedesco' (an address). The relative particle 'ch’' = che introduces a relative clause 'who abandonest' (abbandoni), here 2nd-person singular present (Dante uses the -i ending to address the person directly). 'costei' is a demonstrative pronoun 'this one' (feminine) referring back to the woman/thing being spoken of; 'ch’ è fatta' uses the copula è + past participle fatta to express a passive/resulting state ('has been made' / 'is made'), followed by the adjectives 'indomita e selvaggia' placed after the participle in the usual poetic order.
segments.purgatorio-6-g120: 'e dovresti' is the 2nd-person singular conditional ('you should / ought to'); it is followed by the infinitive 'inforcar' (an archaic/plain infinitive form equivalent to modern inforcare/inforcare) meaning 'to mount' (literally to set onto the pommel). The direct object is 'li suoi arcioni' ('the/its saddle-bows'); note the archaic article 'li' and the possessive agreeing with the feminine demonstrative from the previous clause.
segments.purgatorio-6-g121: Noun phrase 'giusto giudicio' ('just/right judgment') is the subject of an optative/jussive clause. 'da le stelle' is an ablative/agent-like phrase 'from/by the stars' (astrological authority). 'caggia' is an archaic third-person present subjunctive/jussive of a verb meaning 'to fall' (Dante uses this form to express a wish: 'may ... fall'). The prepositional phrase 'sovra ’l tuo sangue' ('upon your blood') specifies the object/target of that wished judgment; note the elided article in '’l' = 'il'.
segments.purgatorio-6-g122: The conjunction 'e' links this clause to the previous wish. 'sia' is the present subjunctive third-person singular of essere used jussively ('may it be'); its grammatical subject is the implied 'giusto giudicio' from the previous clause. 'novo e aperto' are predicate adjectives describing that judgment—'new' and 'open/manifest'—and their postverbal position is normal in poetic/archaic Italian.
segments.purgatorio-6-g123: 'tal che' introduces a result clause 'such that'; '’l tuo successor' is the subject 'your successor'. 'temenza' is a noun 'fear' that functions as the object of the clause. The clitic 'n’' = ne ('of it' / 'from it') refers back to the judgment (i.e. 'fear of it'); 'aggia' is an archaic subjunctive form (parallel to modern 'abbia') used jussively to complete the wish/result ('may your successor have fear of it').

sentences.purgatorio-6-s5: I saw Count Orso and the soul separated from his body by hatred and by envy, as he was saying, not for any fault committed; I mean Pier da la Broccia; and here the lady of Brabant provides (acts) while she is on this side, so that she may not belong to a worse flock.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s6: When I was freed from all those shades who prayed only that others should pray for them, so that they might thereby become saints, I began: «It seems that you deny me, O my light, as is expressed in some text that prayer bends the decree of heaven; and these people pray only for this: would their hope therefore be vain, or is not your saying to me plainly manifest?»
sentences.purgatorio-6-s8: And he to me: «My writing is plain; and the hope of those people does not fail, if it is carefully considered with a sound mind; for the summit of judgment does not give way so that the fire of love should accomplish in a single instant what those who tarry here must satisfy; and where I fixed that point, the defect could not be amended by praying, because that plea was severed from God.»
sentences.purgatorio-6-s17: Still, Virgil drew himself toward her, begging that she show us the best ascent of it; and she did not answer his question, but asked us about our country and about our life; and the sweet guide began, “Mantua.”
sentences.purgatorio-6-s20: “”, and the shade, wholly withdrawn into itself, rose toward him from the place where it had been before, saying: “O Mantuan, I am Sordello of your land!”
sentences.purgatorio-6-s13: Before he is up there, you will see return the one who already covers himself with the mountainside, so that you cannot break his rays.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s14: But behold there a soul who, standing all alone, looks toward us; that one will show us the more arduous way.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s15: We came to her: O Lombard soul, how haughty and disdainful you stood, and in the movement of your eyes dignified and slow!
sentences.purgatorio-6-s16: She said nothing to us, but let us go on, only gazing, in the manner of a lion when it rests.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s9: Truly, do not stop at so lofty a suspicion,
sentences.purgatorio-6-s10: I don't know whether you understand: I mean Beatrice; you will see her above, on the summit of this mountain, laughing and happy.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s11: And I: 'Sir, let's go with greater haste, for I am not already so tired as before, and you see now that the hill casts the shadow.'
sentences.purgatorio-6-s12: 'We shall go on with this day before us,' he answered, 'as much as we can now; but the matter is of a different kind and will not allow delay.'
sentences.purgatorio-6-s34: Come and see how the people love one another!
sentences.purgatorio-6-s35: And if pity for us moves you not at all, shame about your reputation will come upon you.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s36: And if it is permitted to me, O highest Jove, who were crucified on earth for us, are your righteous eyes turned elsewhere?
sentences.purgatorio-6-s21: And the one embraced the other.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s22: Ah, enslaved Italy, hostel of sorrow, a ship without a helmsman in a great storm, not a lady of provinces but a brothel!
sentences.purgatorio-6-s23: That noble soul was so ready — only from the dear sound of his homeland — to make a feast there for his fellow citizen; and now in you your living people do not stand without war, and one gnaws at the other for those whom a wall and a ditch enclose.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s24: Search, wretched one, around your coasts and then look into your breast to see whether any part of you enjoys peace.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s1: When the game of zara breaks up, the one who loses remains sorrowful, repeating the rounds and learning sadly; with the other all the people go away: some go in front, some take him from behind, and some from the side bring them to mind; he does not stop, and he pays attention to this one and that; to whomever he extends his hand he no longer makes haste; and thus he defends himself from the crush.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s2: Such was I in that dense throng, turning my face to them here and there, and by promising I was freed from it.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s3: There was the Aretine who, by the fierce arms of Ghin di Tacco, met his death; and the other who drowned while running in a hunt.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s4: There prayed Federico Novello with outstretched hands, and that man from Pisa who made the good Marzucco appear strong.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s41: Many refuse the common charge; but your people, eager, answers without being called, and cries, "I'll take it upon myself!"
sentences.purgatorio-6-s43: Now it makes you glad, for you have good cause: you are rich, you with peace and you with wisdom!
sentences.purgatorio-6-s44: If I tell the truth, the result does not conceal it.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s29: What have you and your father endured, driven by greed over there, that the garden of the empire should be deserted.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s30: Come see the Montecchi and the Cappelletti, Monaldi and Filippeschi; a man without care: some already sorrowful, and others full of suspicion!
sentences.purgatorio-6-s31: Come, cruel one, come, and see the pressure of your nobles, and mind their faults; and you will see Santafior how dark it is!
sentences.purgatorio-6-s32: Come see your Rome that weeps, widowed and alone; and day and night calls out: 'My Caesar, why do you not accompany me?'
sentences.purgatorio-6-s37: Or is it a preparation that you make in the abyss of your counsel for some good, entirely cut off from our perception?
sentences.purgatorio-6-s38: For the cities of Italy are all full of tyrants, and every peasant who comes taking sides becomes a Marcel.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s39: My Florence, you can well be content with this digression that does not concern you, thanks to your people who argue among themselves.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s40: Many have justice in their hearts, and it shoots late because they will not come to the bow without counsel; but your people have it at the very tip of their mouths.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s45: Athens and Lacedaemon, which made the ancient laws and were so civic, gave a small sign toward living well in your direction; you who make such subtle provisions that by mid-November what you spin in October does not arrive.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s46: How many times, in the time that I remember, have you changed law, coin, office and custom—have you altered them—and renewed your limbs!
sentences.purgatorio-6-s47: And if you well remember and see the light, you will see yourself like that sick woman who cannot find rest on her feathers, but by turning she shields her sorrow.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s25: What good is it — even if Justinian had mended your bridle — if the saddle is empty?
sentences.purgatorio-6-s26: Were it not for it, the shame would be less.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s27: Alas, you people who ought to be devout and to let Caesar sit in the seat — if you rightly understand what God points out to you, see how this beast has become wicked for not being corrected by the spurs, since you put your hand to the predella.
sentences.purgatorio-6-s28: O Albert the German, who abandonest her — she who has been made untameable and wild — and oughtest to mount her saddle-bows, may just judgment from the stars fall upon your blood, and be new and manifest, such that your successor may have fear of it!
